title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Forced Displacement
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy helps survivors of forced displacement heal trauma trapped in the body and restore nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Forced Displacement and Trauma

Forced displacement—whether due to war, persecution, natural disaster, or political upheaval—shatters people’s lives. Millions worldwide endure the loss of home, community, and safety, often experiencing violence, deprivation, and profound uncertainty along the way. The trauma inflicted in these circumstances extends beyond memory; it imprints itself somatically, creating chronic patterns of nervous system dysregulation and physical tension. Survivors frequently find themselves stuck in survival mode, even after reaching safety, unable to fully process the trauma or reclaim a sense of bodily safety.

Traditional talk therapies, while valuable, often fall short for these survivors because trauma is not just a psychological event—it is embodied. As Bessel van der Kolk highlights in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma lives in the body and impacts how survivors experience themselves and the world. Somatic therapy offers an essential approach by addressing these embodied experiences directly.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is a body-centered therapeutic approach that helps individuals access, feel, and release trauma stored in the body. Unlike therapies focused solely on cognition or verbal processing, somatic therapy works with the nervous system’s physiology—using movement, breath awareness, touch, and body scanning techniques to promote regulation and healing.

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes that trauma results from the body’s incomplete defensive responses to overwhelm. When fight, flight, or freeze responses are thwarted or blocked, energy remains trapped in the body, causing symptoms like anxiety, hypervigilance, numbness, and physical pain. Somatic therapy facilitates the completion of these defensive responses, helping the nervous system restore equilibrium.

The Nervous System and Forced Displacement

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a roadmap for understanding how forced displacement affects the nervous system. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) has three primary states:

  • Ventral Vagal: The social engagement system, promoting connection and calm.
  • Sympathetic Activation: Mobilization for fight or flight.
  • Dorsal Vagal: Immobilization or shutdown response to extreme threat.

Survivors of forced displacement often experience prolonged sympathetic activation or dorsal vagal shutdown from chronic threats to safety. This can manifest as hyperarousal, dissociation, or shutdown, which undermine efforts to rebuild a stable sense of self and environment. Somatic therapy targets these states by helping individuals sense when they are in a dysregulated state and develop skills to return to ventral vagal regulation.

Why Somatic Therapy Is Especially Effective for Survivors of Forced Displacement

Survivors carry trauma not just in their memories but in their bodies—through tension, posture, breath patterns, and physiological reactivity. Somatic therapy offers several advantages:

  • Nonverbal Access: Many survivors have language barriers or difficulty articulating trauma. Somatic methods bypass these limits by working directly with felt experience.
  • Restoring Safety: Traumatic displacement disrupts a fundamental sense of safety. Somatic therapy rebuilds safety from the inside out by teaching nervous system regulation skills.
  • Completing Defensive Responses: The therapy helps release frozen survival energy that verbal therapies alone cannot access.
  • Integration of Mind and Body: It supports reconnecting survivors to their bodies, often estranged due to trauma.

Core Somatic Techniques Used in Therapy

Somatic therapists tailor techniques to individual needs but often incorporate:

  • Body Awareness Exercises: Mindful attention to breath, posture, muscle tension, and sensation.
  • Pendulation: Moving attention between states of activation and calm to build tolerance for distress.
  • Resource Building: Identifying internal and external anchors of safety and strength.
  • Movement and Breath: Gentle movement or breath work to discharge trapped energy.
  • Touch and Somatic Tracking: In some modalities, safe touch is used to increase body awareness and regulation, always with consent and cultural sensitivity.

Research and Clinical Evidence

Research supports somatic therapy’s effectiveness for trauma survivors, including those affected by forced displacement. A 2017 meta-analysis in the Journal of Traumatic Stress found that somatic therapies significantly reduce PTSD symptoms. Peter Levine’s work with refugees, as well as programs incorporating Polyvagal-informed practices (Deb Dana), demonstrate improvements in nervous system regulation, emotional resilience, and post-traumatic growth.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, integrating somatic and attachment theories, has also shown promise in helping displaced individuals rebuild boundaries and embodied safety. Similarly, Gabor Maté emphasizes the necessity of addressing bodily trauma in addiction and stress-related conditions common in displaced populations.

Challenges and Considerations

While somatic therapy is powerful, it is not a silver bullet. Challenges include:

  • Cultural Sensitivity: Therapists must adapt approaches to respect cultural understandings of the body and trauma.
  • Safety and Trust: Survivors who have experienced extreme violation may initially find body awareness triggering.
  • Access and Resources: Forced displaced populations often face barriers to consistent therapy access.
  • Integration with Other Supports: Somatic therapy works best alongside social, legal, and community support systems.

Self-Directed Somatic Practices for Survivors

For those unable to access therapy or seeking to complement it, self-directed somatic practices can support healing:

  • Breath Awareness: Slow, deep breaths stimulate the ventral vagal system and promote calm.
  • Grounding Techniques: Feeling feet on the floor or noticing physical contact with a chair can anchor the nervous system.
  • Gentle Movement: Stretching or walking mindfully helps release physical tension.
  • Tracking Sensations: Noticing areas of tension or comfort without judgment builds body connection.

These practices are informed by experts like Dan Siegel, who stresses the importance of mindful presence, and Francine Shapiro’s work on integrating traumatic memories.

"Trauma is not just an event that took place in the past; it is also the imprint left by that experience on mind, body, and nervous system." – Bessel van der Kolk

Conclusion: A Path Toward Reclamation and Resilience

Somatic therapy offers survivors of forced displacement a path toward reclaiming their bodies, restoring nervous system balance, and transforming trauma into resilience. By recognizing trauma as an embodied experience, this approach meets survivors where talk therapy often cannot. With the support of skilled practitioners and informed self-care, survivors can begin to feel safe within themselves again—laying the foundation for healing and rebuilding their lives.

faqs.q: What makes somatic therapy different from traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body and nervous system rather than just thoughts and emotions. It helps release trauma stored in bodily sensations and patterns, which talk therapy alone may not access.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help if I don’t speak the language of my therapist well?
faqs.a: Yes. Because somatic therapy works through bodily sensations and nonverbal cues, it can be effective across language barriers, making it accessible for many forced displacement survivors.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy safe for people with complex trauma from displacement?
faqs.a: When delivered by trained, culturally sensitive therapists, somatic therapy is safe. It emphasizes pacing, consent, and building internal resources to avoid retraumatization.

faqs.q: What are some simple somatic exercises I can try on my own?
faqs.a: Try slow, deep breathing; mindful noticing of your body sensations; gentle stretching; or grounding by feeling your feet on the floor. These help calm the nervous system and reconnect you to your body.
